revision.comment: Bot: tag page without wikidata link [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24796114356|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text: {{日本の氏族 |家名 = Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko |家紋 = |家紋名称 = |本姓 = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} |家祖 = {{ill|Ōtaki-no-Atai|ja|大瀧直|lt=Ōtomo-no-Atai Ōtaki|lt_ja=大伴直大瀧}} |種別 = {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別}} ({{ill|Tenson|ja|天孫}}) |出身地 = |根拠地 = Awa Province (later {{ill|Awa District|ja|安房郡}} and {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平郡|lt=Heguri District|lt_ja=平群郡}} of {{ill|Awa Province|ja|安房国}}) |人物 = See [[#Descendants|Descendants]] |支流 = }} '''Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (''awa no kuni no miyatsuko'', ''awa kokuzō'') was a {{ill|kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} who ruled the region that later became the western part of {{ill|Awa Province|ja|安房国}} ('''[[#Territory|Awa Province]]'''). == Overview == === Name === In the "{{ill|Kokuzō Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}" of the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' it is written '''Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (阿波国造); in the ''{{ill|Tōin Family Records|ja|洞院家記}}'' and ''{{ill|Hokuzansho|ja|北山抄}}'' it is also written '''Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (安房国造). Under the {{ill|ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, because the writing "Awa Province" (阿波国) was used for the old {{ill|Awa Province|ja|粟国}} of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} ({{ill|Awa Province|ja|阿波国}}), the place name Awa of the Tōkaidō, which the Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko ruled, came to be written as 安房 ({{ill|Awa Province|ja|安房国}}, {{ill|Awa District|ja|安房郡}}, etc.). Accordingly, the Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko came to be written as '''Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (安房国造). According to the entry for Kashō 3 ({{ill|850|ja|850年}}) 6th month kinoto-yū (3rd day) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}'' (see {{Harvnb|Shintō/Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}), a person named Tomo-no-Atai Chifukumaro was "Kuni no Miyatsuko of Awa Province". Also, according to the Ijimi no Kuni no Miyatsuko entry in the "{{ill|Kokuzō Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}" of the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'', a person named Ikohoto-no-Mikoto is said to be the ancestor of the "Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko". However, the reading "Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko" in this Ijimi no Kuni no Miyatsuko entry is the reading in the Anpō-period old manuscript held by the Marquess Maeda family{{refnest|group="注"|name=maeda|The {{ill|Maeda clan|ja|前田氏}} {{ill|Kaga Maeda family|ja|前田氏#前田利家の前田氏|lt=Kaga Maeda family|lt_ja=加賀前田家}} main house of the {{ill|Kaga Domain|ja|加賀藩}} became {{ill|marquesses|ja|侯爵#日本の侯爵|lt=marquesses|lt_ja=侯爵}} after the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}.''Kansei Chōshū Shokafu'', ''Kanazawa-shi Shi'', ''Hanshi Daijiten''}} and others; in the {{ill|Jingū Library|ja|神宮文庫}} copy it is written "'''A-do-no-Kuni-no-Miyatsuko'''" (安度国造).''{{ill|Shinteizōho Kokushi Taikei Vol. 7|ja|#taikei|lt=Shinteizōho Kokushi Taikei Vol. 7|lt_ja=新訂増補國史大系 第7巻}}'' According to the ''{{ill|Tōin Family Records|ja|洞院家記}}'' and ''{{ill|Hokuzanshō|ja|北山抄}}'', the continued existence of the Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko is confirmed up to around the {{ill|10th century|ja|10世紀}}.{{Sfn|Awa Province (Heibonsha)|1996}}{{Sfn|Chiba-ken no Rekishi Tsūshi-hen Kodai 2|2001|pp=604-612}}{{Sfn|Kawajiri Akio|2003|pp=70-117}} === Ancestry === ==== Mononobe / Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko line ==== *According to the "{{ill|Kokuzō Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}" of the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'', during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}} (the 13th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|lt=Emperor|lt_ja=天皇}}), '''{{ill|Ōtomo-no-Ōtaki|ja|大伴大瀧}}''', grandson of Mitsurogi-no-Mikoto — who was an 8th-generation descendant of '''{{ill|Amenohohi|ja|天穂日命|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=天穂日命}}''' — was appointed the first Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko. *Also, in the Ijimi no Kuni no Miyatsuko entry of the same "Kokuzō Hongi" of the ''Sendai Kuji Hongi'', it is stated that during the reign of Emperor Seimu, {{ill|Ikorotо-no-Atai|ja|伊己侶止|lt=Ikorotо-no-Atai|lt_ja=伊己侶止直}} ({{ill|Atai|ja|直 (姓)|lt=Atai|lt_ja=直}} being a {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|lt=kabane|lt_ja=姓}}), grandson of Ikohoto-no-Mikoto — the ancestor of the Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko — was appointed the first Ijimi no Kuni no Miyatsuko. However, the reading "Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko" in this Ijimi no Kuni no Miyatsuko entry is that of the Anpō-period old manuscript held by the Marquess Maeda family, and in the Jingū Library copy it is written "'''A-do-no-Kuni-no-Miyatsuko'''" (安度国造). === Clan === '''{{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}''' (''ōtomo-uji''; {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|lt=kabane|lt_ja=姓}} of {{ill|Atai (kabane)|ja|直 (姓)|lt=Atai|lt_ja=直}}), of the same lineage as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}}, {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}}, and others. Locally they led the {{ill|bemin|ja|部民}} clan {{ill|Kashiwade-no-Ōtomobe|ja|膳大伴部}} (''kashiwade-no-ōtomobe''; Ōtomobe) in charge of provisioning the emperor's table (especially offering {{ill|abalone|ja|アワビ}}); they are also called '''{{ill|Kashiwade-no-Ōtomo clan|ja|膳大伴氏}}''' (kabane of Atai). In {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}} 14 ({{ill|823|ja|823年}}) the Ōtomo clan changed to '''Tomo clan''' (kabane of Atai).''{{ill|Seishi Kakei Daijiten Vol. 1|ja|#seishi|lt=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Vol. 1|lt_ja=姓氏家系大辞典. 第1巻}}''. (See also {{ill|Ōtomo clan#Political Involvement and Decline|ja|大伴氏#政争への関与と衰退}}.) Among clans that also changed from the Ōtomo clan to the Tomo clan is the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (kabane of {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} and later {{ill|Sukune|ja|宿禰}}), later the Tomo clan (kabane of Sukune and later {{ill|Ason|ja|朝臣}}) — an influential {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}} clan thought to have managed the {{ill|military|ja|軍事}} of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|lt=Imperial Court|lt_ja=朝廷}} together with the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}; the {{ill|Ōtomobe|ja|大伴部}} may refer to the ''be'' managed by this Ōtomo clan. == Base == The later {{ill|Awa District|ja|安房郡}} of {{ill|Awa Province|ja|安房国}}''{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chizu: Primitive and Ancient Period Vol. 2|ja|#chizu|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chizu: Primitive and Ancient Period Vol. 2|lt_ja=日本歴史地図　原始・古代編　下}}''. — present-day {{ill|Tateyama, Chiba|ja|館山市|lt=Tateyama City|lt_ja=館山市}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}. The position of {{ill|district magistrate|ja|郡司|lt=gunji|lt_ja=郡司}} of Awa District is thought to have been held by the Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko (Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko) family.{{Sfn|Awa Province (Heibonsha)|1996}}{{Sfn|Chiba-ken no Rekishi Tsūshi-hen Kodai 2|2001|pp=604-612}}{{Sfn|Chiba-ken no Rekishi (Yamakawa)|2012|pp=33-35}} === Territory === The Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko's territory was the region then called '''Awa Province''' (阿波国). Awa Province refers to the western part of the later {{ill|ritsuryō province|ja|令制国}} of {{ill|Awa Province|ja|安房国}} ({{ill|Awa District|ja|安房郡}} and {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平郡|lt=Heguri District|lt_ja=平群郡}}), corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tateyama, Chiba|ja|館山市|lt=Tateyama City|lt_ja=館山市}} and parts of {{ill|Kyonan, Chiba|ja|鋸南町}} in {{ill|Awa District|ja|安房郡}} and {{ill|Minamibōsō, Chiba|ja|南房総市}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}. Later, the northern part of Awa Province became {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平郡|lt=Heguri District|lt_ja=平群郡}} and the southern part became {{ill|Awa District|ja|安房郡|lt=Awa District|lt_ja=阿波郡}}. Further, in the area corresponding to the eastern part of the later Awa Province ({{ill|Nagasa District|ja|長狭郡}} and {{ill|Asahina District|ja|朝夷郡}}) — present-day {{ill|Kamogawa, Chiba|ja|鴨川市}} and parts of {{ill|Minamibōsō, Chiba|ja|南房総市}} — was Nagasa Province, ruled by the {{ill|Nagasa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|長狭国造}}, though this kuni no miyatsuko is not recorded in the "Kokuzō Hongi" of the ''Sendai Kuji Hongi''. This is thought to be because Nagasa Province bordered on the north with the {{ill|Ijimi Miyake|ja|伊甚屯倉}} seen in the 4th month entry of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇}} 1 ({{ill|534|ja|534年}}) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', and was also pressed from the south by the Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko, so that the Nagasa no Kuni no Miyatsuko lost its power by the {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}. The origin of the place name Awa, according to the ''{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}'', is that {{ill|Ame-no-Tomi-no-Mikoto|ja|天富命}} — who built {{ill|Kashihara-no-Miya|ja|橿原神宮|lt=Kashihara-no-Miya|lt_ja=橿原宮}} in the eastern expedition of {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}} (the first {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|lt=Emperor|lt_ja=天皇}}) ({{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}) — during the same emperor's reign[http://www.nihonjiten.com/data/263219.html Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko (Awa)] - Nihon Jiten (accessed 2017-12-12 at 01:14 ([[JST|JST]])), led the {{ill|Inbe clan|ja|忌部氏}} of {{ill|Awa Province|ja|阿波国|lt=Awa|lt_ja=阿波}}Before the {{ill|Wadō|ja|和銅}} 6 ({{ill|713|ja|713年}}) favorable-character edict, it was written "粟". of Shikoku in search of more fertile land, rode the {{ill|Kuroshio|ja|黒潮}} to this region, and named the residence of the Awa Inbe "Awa" after the place name of their original home. Awa Province and some ten-odd provinces were called {{ill|Fusa Province|ja|総国|lt=Fusa Province (Kuni)|lt_ja=総国（捄国）}}; according to the ''Kogo Shūi'', because the hemp planted by Ame-no-Tomi-no-Mikoto grew well, the name "Fusa Province" (or, by one theory, "Fusa-dō") was given, from "''fusa''", another name for hemp. The ''{{ill|Takahashi-uji Bumi|ja|高橋氏文}}'' states that on the occasion of the eastern tour by {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} (the 12th Emperor), {{ill|Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto|ja|磐鹿六雁命}} was granted the Kashiwade-no-Ōtomobe and his descendants made chiefs of Kazusa Province and '''Awa Province'''The ''Takahashi-uji Bumi'' treats {{ill|Awa Shrine|ja|安房神社}} as a Miketsu-Kami shrine. For this reason, there is also a view regarding Awa Province as a {{ill|Miketsukuni|ja|御食国}}., so it is clear that in this period the area was called Awa Province (淡国). Regarding the record of this eastern tour, the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' mentions not Awa Province but {{ill|Awa Water Gate|ja|淡水門}} (entry for Emperor Keikō 53, 10th month), and there is also a theory that the name of Awa Province derives from this.''Nihon Kodaishi Chimei Jiten'', p. 228. Awa Province and 11 other provinces (Awa Province, {{ill|Nagasa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|長狭国造|lt=Nagasa Province|lt_ja=長狭国}}, {{ill|Sue no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|須恵国造|lt=Sue Province|lt_ja=須恵国}}, {{ill|Mukuda no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|馬来田国造|lt=Mukuda Province|lt_ja=馬来田国}}, {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|菊麻国造|lt=Kikuma Province|lt_ja=菊麻国}}, {{ill|Ijimi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊甚国造|lt=Ijimi Province|lt_ja=伊甚国}}, {{ill|Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|lt=Kami-tsu-Unakami Province|lt_ja=上海上国}}, {{ill|Muza no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武社国造|lt=Muza Province|lt_ja=武社国}}, {{ill|Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|下海上国造|lt=Shimotsu-Unakami Province|lt_ja=下海上国}}, {{ill|Chiba no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|千葉国造|lt=Chiba Province|lt_ja=千葉国}}, {{ill|Inba no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|印波国造|lt=Inba Province|lt_ja=印波国}}) were called {{ill|Fusa Province|ja|総国|lt=Fusa Province (Kuni)|lt_ja=総国（捄国）}}. In {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇}} 1 ({{ill|534|ja|534年}}) (according to the ''{{ill|Teiō Hennenki|ja|帝王編年記}}''), 8 provinces of Fusa Province including Awa Province (Awa, Nagasa, Sue, Mukuda, Kikuma, Ijimi, Kami-tsu-Unakami, Muza) split off to form {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}} (Upper Fusa Province). Regarding the timing of this split, because it takes the "Upper" form like Kōzuke Province split from {{ill|Keno Province|ja|毛野|lt=Keno Province|lt_ja=毛野国}}, some argue for the mid-{{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}.Kusuhara Yūsuke et al. (eds.), ''Kodai Chimei Gogen Jiten'', entry on "Fusa", {{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}, 1981. ISBN 4-490-10148-1 Kazusa Province became a {{ill|ritsuryō province|ja|令制国}} in the {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}. On {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 2, 5th month, 2nd day (old calendar) ({{ill|June 4, 718|ja|718年}}[[6月4日]]), 4 districts — {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平郡|lt=Heguri District|lt_ja=平群郡}} and {{ill|Awa District|ja|安房郡}} (these 2 districts being the old Awa Province) plus {{ill|Asahina District|ja|朝夷郡}} and {{ill|Nagasa District|ja|長狭郡}} (these 2 districts being the old {{ill|Nagasa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|長狭国造|lt=Nagasa Province|lt_ja=長狭国}}) — were split off from Kazusa Province, and Awa Province was newly established, but on {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 13, 12th month, 10th day (old calendar) ({{ill|January 20, 742|ja|742年}}[[1月20日]]), Awa Province was again merged into Kazusa Province. Thereafter, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 ({{ill|757|ja|757年}}), Awa Province was re-established with the original 4 districts. == Tutelary Shrine == The {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} of the Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko is '''{{ill|Awa Shrine|ja|安房神社}}''' (''awa jinja'', {{Coord|34|55|20.80|N|139|50|12.25|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark|name=Awa Shrine}}), the {{ill|ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Awa Province|ja|安房国}} located in {{ill|Tateyama, Chiba|ja|館山市|lt=Tateyama City|lt_ja=館山市}} (former {{ill|Awa District|ja|安房郡}}) in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}. This shrine enshrines as principal deity {{ill|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}}, the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Inbe clan|ja|忌部氏}} (see [[#Ancestry|#Ancestry]]), and is said to have been founded by that clan. The deity of this shrine, Awa-no-Kami, is thought to have been the deity worshiped by the Kashiwade-no-Ōtomo clan (Ōtomo/Tomo clans; the clan of the Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko. See [[#Clan|#Clan]]),{{Sfn|Awa Inbe (Chiba Daihyakka)|1982}}{{Sfn|Awa Province (Heibonsha)|1996}}{{Sfn|Chiba-ken no Rekishi Tsūshi-hen Kodai 2|2001|pp=604-612}} and the rituals of this shrine are thought to have been carried out by the Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko (Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko) family.{{Sfn|Awa Province (Heibonsha)|1996}}{{Sfn|Chiba-ken no Rekishi Tsūshi-hen Kodai 2|2001|pp=604-612}}{{Sfn|Chiba-ken no Rekishi (Yamakawa)|2012|pp=33-35}} === Tombs === == People == Prominent figures who served as Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko are listed below. * '''{{ill|Tomo-no-Chifukumaro|ja|伴千福麻呂}}''' *: {{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ|lt=kabane|lt_ja=姓}} of {{ill|Atai (kabane)|ja|直 (姓)|lt=Atai|lt_ja=直}}. Said to have been "Kuni no Miyatsuko of Awa Province". He was {{ill|Senior Eighth Rank Upper|ja|正八位上}}, but on {{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉祥}} 3 ({{ill|850|ja|850年}}) 6th month 3rd day (old calendar) he was granted {{ill|Outer Junior Fifth Rank Lower|ja|外従五位下}}.''{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}'', entry for Kashō 3 ({{ill|850|ja|850年}}) 6th month kinoto-yū (3rd day) == Descendants == The following two are presumed to be members of the Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko family.{{Sfn|Awa Province (Heibonsha)|1996}}{{Sfn|Awa District (Heibonsha)|1996}} * '''{{ill|Ōtomo-no-Tomi Katsumaro|ja|大伴登美勝麻呂}}''' (''ōtomo-no-tomi no katsumaro'') *: A person of Awa Province, {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank Lower|ja|正六位下}}. Originally of the Ōtomo clan ({{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|lt=kabane|lt_ja=姓}} of {{ill|Atai (kabane)|ja|直 (姓)|lt=Atai|lt_ja=直}}), he was granted the Ōtomo-Tomi surname ({{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|lt=kabane|lt_ja=姓}} of {{ill|Sukune|ja|宿禰}}) on {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}} 2 ({{ill|811|ja|811年}}) 3rd month 6th day (old calendar).''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}}'', entry for Kōnin 2 ({{ill|811|ja|811年}}) 3rd month kōshi (6th day) * '''{{ill|Tomo-no-Yakanushi|ja|伴家主}}''' (''tomo no yakanushi'', ''tomo no ienushi'') *: A person of Awa District. For his filial piety, on {{ill|Jōwa (Japan)|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Jōwa|lt_ja=承和}} 3 ({{ill|836|ja|836年}}) 12th month 7th day (old calendar), he was recognized as a filial son and granted three ranks by {{ill|Imperial edict|ja|勅#日本|lt=imperial edict|lt_ja=勅}}, and his {{ill|household|ja|戸 (律令制)|lt=household|lt_ja=戸}}'s {{ill|land tax|ja|田租}} was exempted for life, and he was honored at the gates of his house and the entrances of his village.''{{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}'', entry for Jōwa 3 ({{ill|836|ja|836年}}) 12th month shinchū (7th day) The "Kōshizuka" in Kokubun of {{ill|Tateyama, Chiba|ja|館山市|lt=Tateyama City|lt_ja=館山市}}, a city {{ill|historic site|ja|史跡}}, is said to be Yakanushi's tomb. == Examination by Sakimoto Masanori == {{ill|Sakimoto Masanori|ja|崎元正教}} takes the stance that what is recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' is not merely {{ill|legend|ja|伝承}}, and adds examinations from shrine traditions: he points out that the traditions about Ame-no-Tomi-no-Mikoto spanning multiple {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}} including {{ill|Awa Shrine|ja|安房神社}} can each be explained without contradiction, and evaluates their credibility.''Yamato Takeru ni Himerareta Kodaishi'', pp. 69-76 Regarding Emperor Keikō's eastern tour, from an examination of shrine traditions and the like, he argues that he went via {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|lt=Ise|lt_ja=伊勢}}, then from {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|lt=Owari|lt_ja=尾張}} by sea route to {{ill|Bōsō|ja|房総}}''Yamato Takeru ni Himerareta Kodaishi'', p. 87; the area had deep connections with {{ill|Kinai|ja|畿内}} and other {{ill|western provinces|ja|西国}} from before the {{ill|Japanese ritsuryō system|ja|律令制#日本の律令制|lt=Japanese ritsuryō system|lt_ja=律令制}} by {{ill|water transport|ja|水上交通}} using the {{ill|Kuroshio|ja|黒潮}} and the Kuroshio counterflow. Additionally, many common place names between the present {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, and southern Bōsō are well known. == Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === Citations === {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Katō Kenkichi et al. (eds.), ''Nihon Kodaishi Chimei Jiten'', Yuzankaku, 2007, ISBN 978-4-639-01995-4. * Sakimoto Masanori, ''Yamato Takeru ni Himerareta Kodaishi'', Keyaki Shuppan, 2005, ISBN 4-87751-287-X. * Ogasawara Nagakazu (supervisor), {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1996|chapter=|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 12: Place Names of Chiba Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490123|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Awa Province"|ref={{Harvid|Awa Province (Heibonsha)|1996}}}}. * {{Cite book|和書|author=|year=2001|title=Chiba-ken no Rekishi Tsūshi-hen Kodai 2 (Kenshi Series 2)|publisher=Chiba Prefecture|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Chiba-ken no Rekishi Tsūshi-hen Kodai 2|2001}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kawajiri Akio|authorlink=Kawajiri Akio|year=2003|chapter=The Characteristics of Ancient Awa Province: The Great Deity of Awa and the Kashiwade-no-Kami|title=Kodai Tōgokushi no Kisoteki Kenkyū|publisher=Hanawa Shobō|isbn=4827311803|ref={{Harvid|Kawajiri Akio|2003}}}} * {{Cite web|和書|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/170101.html|author=|title=Awa-ni-Masu Shrine (Awa District, Awa Province)|work=|publisher=Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shintō/Shrine Historical Materials Collection"|date=|accessdate=2015-11-15|ref={{Harvid|Shintō/Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2012|chapter=|title=Chiba-ken no Rekishi (Kenshi 12) 2nd edition|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa
